perturbation.original_text: If the Fund's Net Investment Income on any day is below zero, the Investment Adviser shall waive its Advisory Fee or reimburse the Fund an amount (defined as "Expense Waiver") sufficient to produce a Net Investment Income of zero.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Fund's Net Investment Income on any day is below zero, the Investment Adviser may consider waiving its Advisory Fee or consider reimbursing the Fund an amount (defined as "Expense Waiver") in order to produce a Net Investment Income that is more than zero.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a waiver or reimbursement to bring the Net Investment Income to zero. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'may consider,' making the waiver discretionary. It also states 'more than zero' which is ambiguous, as zero is also more than zero.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 36(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 imposes a fiduciary duty on investment advisers regarding compensation. By making the waiver or reimbursement discretionary ('may consider'), the modified text weakens the adviser's obligation to maintain the Fund's Net Investment Income, potentially breaching this fiduciary duty to shareholders. The Act implies that the investment advisor should be actively ensuring the investment income is at or above zero by waiving fees or reimbursing the fund.

Breach of fiduciary duty (a) Civil actions by Commission; jurisdiction; allegations; injunctive or other relief The Commission is authorized to bring an action in the proper district court of the United States, or in the United States court of any territory or other place subject to the jurisdiction of the United States, alleging that a person who is, or at the time of the alleged misconduct was,
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the fiduciary duty imposed on investment advisors, as described in the Law Explanation. The original text requires the Investment Advisor to waive fees or reimburse funds to maintain a Net Investment Income of zero. The changed text introduces 'may consider,' making this action discretionary. This weakens the obligation, potentially breaching the fiduciary duty as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition states that a contradiction arises when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce, which aligns with the modification here.

perturbation.original_text: If the Investment Adviser is required to reimburse the Fund, the Investment Adviser shall make such payment within 30 days after each month-end in the amount due the Fund as of each month.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Investment Adviser is expected to reimburse the Fund, the Investment Adviser should attempt to make such payment around 30 days after the end of each month with the proper amount to be determined.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly mandates payment within 30 days. The modified text replaces 'is required' with 'is expected,' 'shall make' with 'should attempt,' and 'within 30 days' with 'around 30 days,' making the obligation and timeframe non-binding and vague. It also adds 'proper amount to be determined', making the reimbursement amount unclear.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 22c-1 under the Investment Company Act of 1940 pertains to the pricing of redeemable securities for distribution, redemption, and repurchase. The specific requirement for prompt and precise payment is crucial for maintaining fairness and preventing dilution of shareholder value. The original clause ensures this consistent valuation; replacing it with a vague timeline contradicts these requirements, making the reimbursement potentially unfair. It introduces ambiguity in the settlement process, affecting the NAV calculation. Making the 'proper amount to be determined' also puts the payment at risk of being incorrect.

CFR prev | next § 270.22c-1 Pricing of redeemable securities for distribution , redemption and repurchase. (a) No registered investment company issuing any redeemable security, no person designated in such issuer 's prospectus as authorized to consummate transactions in any such security, and no principal underwriter of, or dealer in, any such security shall sell, redeem, or repurchase any such security except at a price based on the current net asset value of such security which is next computed after receipt of a tender of such security for redemption or of an order to purchase or sell such security: Provided, That: (1) This paragraph shall not prevent a sponsor of a unit investment trust (hereinafter referred to as the “Trust”) engaged exclusively in the business of investing in eligible trust securities (as defined in Rule 14a-3(b) ( 17 CFR 270.14a-3 (b))) from selling or repurchasing Trust units in a secondary market at a price based on the offering side evaluation of the eligible trust securities in the Trust's portfolio, determined at any time on the last business day of each week, effective for all sales made during the following week, if on the days that such sales or repurchases are made the sponsor receives a letter from a qualified evaluator stating, in its opinion, that: (i) In the case of repurchases, the current bid price is not higher than the offering side evaluation, computed on the last business day of the previous week; and (ii) In the case of resales, the offering side evaluation, computed as of the last business day of the previous week, is not more than one-half of one percent ($5.00 on a unit representing $1,00
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Rule 22c-1 under the Investment Company Act of 1940, which focuses on maintaining fairness and preventing dilution of shareholder value through the prompt and precise payment. The original clause ensures consistent valuation; replacing it with vague timeline contradicts these requirements. The original text mandates the Investment Advisor 'shall make such payment within 30 days', while the changed text uses language that is not binding, such as 'is expected', 'should attempt' and 'around 30 days'. The modified text also makes the amount unclear by adding 'proper amount to be determined', which goes against the original intention of clear and consistent valuation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will terminate upon the termination of the Advisory Agreement except the Investment Adviser's Right of Recovery set forth in section 1.5 herein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may terminate upon the termination of the Advisory Agreement, with the Investment Adviser's Right of Recovery set forth in section 1.5 herein possibly remaining in effect, subject to further determination.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states a clear condition for termination. The modified text introduces uncertainty by replacing 'will terminate' with 'may terminate' and adding 'possibly remaining in effect, subject to further determination'. This creates ambiguity about whether the agreement will automatically terminate or if further actions are required, and about the fate of the Right of Recovery.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a)(4) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires that investment advisory contracts provide for automatic termination upon assignment. By introducing the possibility that the agreement may *not* terminate upon termination of the advisory agreement, the modified text is in direct contradiction of this provision. The statute requires that advisory agreements and any collateral agreements must automatically end when the advisory relationship ceases. Removing the certainty of automatic termination circumvents this clear legal requirement. Furthermore, by making the rights of recovery 'subject to further determination', parties are no longer sure of the validity of the contract's clauses.

Contracts of advisers and underwriters (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by introducing uncertainty regarding the termination of the agreement upon the termination of the Advisory Agreement. The original text states that the agreement 'will terminate,' whereas the changed text says it 'may terminate'. The law explanation points to Section 15(a)(4) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, which requires investment advisory contracts to provide for automatic termination upon assignment. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 15 USC 80a-15, which concerns contracts of advisers and underwriters. The change from 'will' to 'may', coupled with the added uncertainty about the Investment Adviser's Right of Recovery, directly contradicts the requirement for automatic termination, creating ambiguity about the agreement's adherence to the law. The changes introduce ambiguity where the law requires certainty, thus creating a legal contradiction.
